title: #eng: 1738960013
preview:
Connor O'Brien: pulling folks into one thread re: feature flag + cohort mechanics for onboarding checklist MVP. We need to lock this this week for rollout plan + Support readiness. PR is up (pr-28467) but there are open questions.

Dylan Brooks: thx. current implementation in Console uses `onboarding_checklist_enabled` from config service. if false, we hide entrypoint + don’t call progress API. if true, UI loads and renders whatever state.

Chloe Martin: backend side (progress svc) is always on in prod (since gateway can emit events regardless). but we can gate the *Console access* + step details. I’m +1 to not gating event emission; otherwise we create data gaps.

Logan Wright: +1. telemetry wants events regardless so we can baseline funnel / TTFS pre/post. gating event emission by flag breaks comparability. we can add `flag_variant`/`exposure` as context in the events if we need.

Ben Carter: for cohorting, we should decide: tenant-level or user-level. Product asked for “accounts see checklist” not “some users in same org see different things”. I strongly recommend tenant-level.

Marissa Cole: tenant-level is the intent. we don’t want a support scenario where admin sees checklist and other teammate doesn’t.

Nikhil Sharma: agree. also for TTFS/TTFP reporting we care per tenant, so exposure should be stable.

Connor O'Brien: ok tenant-level. question: where do we compute cohort? console side vs server side.

Ethan Park: compute server-side in control plane/config. if Console computes cohort locally you can get drift (different SDK versions, different hashing). Better: config service returns `onboardingChecklist: { enabled: true, variant: "A", cohort: "dogfood" }`.

Dylan Brooks: today we’re calling `GET /v1/console/config` and it returns feature gates already. we can extend.

Talia Benson: also helps with “kill switch” - one place to flip.

Connor O'Brien: cool. what’s the cohort rule? what fields do we use for deterministic bucketing.

Ben Carter: do NOT use email domain. use `tenant_id` stable hash. segmentation rules layered on top:
1) allowlist internal dogfood tenants
2) allowlist a few design partners
3) then % rollout by tenant_id hash bucket
We can also exclude “enterprise” plan if we want, but we need clarity.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE perspective, % rollout must be a real kill switch (0%) and must be fast to propagate (<5 min). also: if there’s an outage in progress svc, the flag should fail closed (hide UI) not degrade console performance.

Chloe Martin: progress svc is behind the same edge as Console API. if it errors, UI can show “Checklist temporarily unavailable” and still render rest of Console. We can also have Console only call it if enabled.

Allison Grant: we should treat it as non-critical path. timeouts should be low (e.g., 300-500ms) + no retries from browser. avoid cascading issues.

Dylan Brooks: yup, I set 400ms timeout and on failure we drop the component (no blocking). we still log a metric + Sentry event.

Nadia Rahman: re: adding `flag_variant` to events: prefer server-side enrichment. gateway emits events; telemetry pipeline can join against exposure dataset keyed by tenant_id + timestamp. otherwise you leak flag evaluation details into multiple emitters.

Logan Wright: +1. we can write an “exposure snapshot” table in analytics daily + also a real-time KV for joins. but simplest: config svc also emits `onboarding.checklist.exposed` event when enabled flips from false->true for a tenant.

Connor O'Brien: do we need an explicit exposure event for counting? or can we just use config snapshot.

Logan Wright: for accurate funnel denominators, explicit exposure event is nice. but we can ship MVP with snapshot and add event later.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with snapshot for MVP as long as we can answer “who saw the checklist” reliably. Support will ask.

Amara Diallo: question: if we don’t gate gateway event emission, do we risk “step complete” happening for tenants who never saw checklist? ex: api key created, first 2xx request, etc.

Chloe Martin: that’s fine. progress state is basically computed from behavior; showing UI is separate. if tenant later gets exposed, their checklist will appear partially complete (which might be good!).

Ben Carter: agree. and it reduces “cold start” confusion.

Nikhil Sharma: BUT: need to make sure step ordering doesn’t look weird if they already did prod stuff. e.g., “Set budget alerts” might be incomplete while “Send production traffic” looks complete.

Dylan Brooks: UX spec has “Recommended next” behavior. If some advanced steps are complete, we still show sequence but highlight the first incomplete. Also can collapse completed steps.

Talia Benson: ok. rollout mechanics: what cohorts for phase 1 dogfood?

Connor O'Brien: proposal:
- Week 1 internal (Redwood tenants) + 3 handpicked partner tenants
- Then 5% new self-serve signups (created_at >= rollout date)
- Then 25% all eligible
Need to decide if we only target *new* tenants or include existing.

Ben Carter: for activation lift we care mainly new. I’d do new-only initially to avoid surprising existing customers in Console. But telemetry wants some existing for baseline comparisons.

Logan Wright: baseline comparisons don’t require exposure; we can compare pre vs post on new signups only. I’m ok with new-only for the UI.

Marissa Cole: new-only simplifies Support training too (“If they signed up after X they probably have it”).

Sean Gallagher: new-only reduces blast radius. do it.

Chloe Martin: implementing “new-only” requires we have tenant.created_at in config service. do we already have that accessible?

Ethan Park: yes in control plane. config svc can evaluate rule `created_at >= 2025-02-xx`.

Dylan Brooks: one nuance: if we gate on created_at, tenants created just before date never get it unless we run a migration/override. maybe we want an allowlist for “requested access”.

Connor O'Brien: we can include an override list. Support asked for this anyway (ENG-5852 admin override is separate though). For rollout gating, we can maintain a “rollout allowlist” in config.

Ben Carter: yes. allowlist should be in control plane, editable by oncall/Release coord.

Allison Grant: please put guardrails: any manual allowlist change should be audited. doesn’t need full SOC2 today but at least who/when.

Ethan Park: we can log changes in config admin logs.

LaunchDarkly Bot: LaunchDarkly flag update: `console.onboarding_checklist` created in project `redwood-console`. Default: off. Rules: allowlist (internal tenants), then 0% rollout.

Dylan Brooks: wait we’re using LaunchDarkly directly? I thought we were keeping feature flags in control plane config.

Connor O'Brien: sorry, to clarify: pr-28467 uses LD SDK server-side in config service. Console reads config service, not LD directly.

Chloe Martin: ok that’s fine.

Nadia Rahman: if LD is used, please ensure config service caches evaluation results (per tenant) w/ TTL. don’t want every console page load hammering LD.

Ethan Park: caching is in place. We cache per tenant_id for 5 minutes; bust cache on config change webhook.

Sean Gallagher: 5 minutes ok. make sure kill switch propagation meets SRE ask. webhook path must be reliable.

Ethan Park: webhook already used for other flags. if webhook fails, worst case TTL 5 min.

Connor O'Brien: decision points to record:
1) tenant-level bucketing
2) config service evaluates flag (LD backed)
3) UI gated, event emission not gated
4) new tenants only initially + allowlist override
5) fail closed if progress svc down (hide component)
Anything else?

Marissa Cole: also: exclude enterprise contracts at first? I’d exclude anything with `plan_tier=enterprise` until Support + CS trained.

Ben Carter: I’d exclude enterprise by default, but allowlist for design partners.

Connor O'Brien: ok. Ethan can add rule in config service.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll update Console to display a small “Beta” pill only for dogfood cohort? or for all. UX spec says avoid “beta” unless we think it’s unstable.

Nikhil Sharma: avoid beta label; it makes customers nervous. use normal UI.

Talia Benson: timeline: can we land the rule changes today so we can start dogfood tomorrow?

Ethan Park: yes if decisions final. I’ll update config rules + add fields returned to Console: `enabled`, `cohort`, `rollout_reason` (for debugging; not shown in UI).

Allison Grant: careful with `rollout_reason` surfacing to client. don’t leak internal segmentation logic. keep it server logs only.

Dylan Brooks: +1. we can keep it behind an internal header or only in logs.

Connor O'Brien: ok. action items:
- Ethan: implement new-only + enterprise exclude + allowlist; keep reason server-side
- Dylan: ensure progress API calls are gated + timeout/fail closed + no retries
- Logan/Nadia: confirm telemetry approach for exposure denominator (snapshot ok MVP)
- Connor: update PM-768 + rollout doc with these decisions

Logan Wright: I’ll post in #eng-platform that telemetry is ok with snapshot for MVP, but we should add an exposure event in phase 2.

Sean Gallagher: and please define kill criteria. If error rate for progress svc > X or console p95 increases by Y, we flip to 0%.

Connor O'Brien: yep will add go/no-go + kill switch thresholds in rollout plan.

Marissa Cole: thanks all. once config is updated, ping me and I’ll sanity check in staging.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

title: #architecture: 1738862109
preview:
Miles Thornton: We need to settle backup artifact format stability + migration strategy before we lock docs. Specifically: do we treat backups as a semver’d API? What are our compatibility guarantees?

Michael Nguyen: +1. ENG-9830 is blocked on this. Today the installer emits `formatVersion: 1` in the manifest but it’s basically “best effort.” If we’re shipping to regulated on-prem, “oops new installer can’t restore old backup” is not acceptable.

Ethan Park: Agree on not breaking restores, but we should be explicit about scope. We’re backing up control-plane state (db + config + some k8s objects) NOT “entire cluster.” That affects what “compatibility” means.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: we can enforce at build time that backup format changes are reviewed (ADR + changelog). But we need a clear policy: what’s MAJOR vs MINOR. Also, we have to support air-gapped bundle verification; migrations can’t require “reach out to Redwood servers.”

Miles Thornton: Let’s enumerate options:
1) Backup format is versioned (semver) and restore supports N previous majors
2) Backup format is “pinned” to platform version and we ship migration utilities
3) We never change backup format (lol)

Hanae Suzuki: Security angle: any migration step that decrypts/re-encrypts is a key-handling event. Need deterministic behavior + audit logs. Also: do we keep old ciphertext as-is and only rewrite manifest? depends on what changes.

Marcus Lin: Also threat model: backups are an attacker target. Format changes often come with metadata changes. We should avoid “silent coercion” on restore. If we detect unknown fields/version, fail closed with actionable message.

Benji Okafor: Practical: restore workflow needs to decide early if it can proceed. I want something like:
- read manifest
- verify checksums
- verify signature (air-gapped)
- check `formatVersion` compatibility
- only then decrypt / import

Michael Nguyen: +1. Can we do semver on backup *format* (manifest schema + archive layout) and separately track *data migrations* (db schema)? They’re related but not the same.

Ethan Park: Yep. DB migrations already exist in control-plane. Restore is trickier because we may restore to a different platform version.

Neha Kapoor: Reliability POV: we should define a contract that makes SRE life easier:
- N-1 restore works (newest installer can restore previous minor/patch backups)
- Major bumps require explicit operator action + preflight warning
- Provide a “dry run / validate” mode that tells you exactly what will happen

Allison Grant: Observability note: we need structured events when format mismatch happens (category: compatibility) so dashboards can show how often customers are hitting it.

Miles Thornton: Ok, semver proposal:
- `backupFormatVersion` = MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
- MAJOR: breaking layout/schema change (requires migration tool)
- MINOR: backwards compatible additions (new fields optional)
- PATCH: bugfix / clarifications (no behavior change)

Marcus Lin: I’m mostly ok with semver, but define “backwards compatible” rigorously. Example: adding new metadata is fine if restore ignores unknown keys. But do we *want* ignoring unknown keys? That’s the classic footgun.

Michael Nguyen: On restore we can do: allow unknown keys in manifest but require known `backupFormatVersion` range. Unknown keys get logged (debug). If MAJOR mismatched, error. If MINOR higher than supported, error unless we can safely ignore.

Ethan Park: That turns into: restore supports `MAJOR == X` and `MINOR <= maxMinorSupported`. That’s fine.

Connor O'Brien: But you’re describing “new backup might not restore on older installer,” which is ok. We care more about “new installer restoring old backup.”

Neha Kapoor: Exactly. Customer expectation: you can upgrade tooling and still restore the last few backups.

Miles Thornton: So compatibility direction: backward compat for restore (new tool restores old backups). Forward compat (old tool restores new backup) not required.

Hanae Suzuki: With encryption: if we change encryption envelope format, that’s a MAJOR. But we should isolate encryption metadata in manifest and keep ciphertext opaque. Ideally: encryption is “provider + parameters” and doesn’t change often.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also: manifest should include KMS key reference (arn/uri) but never include plaintext material. And we should not back up actual secret values if we can avoid it; references only (aligns w/ ADR-027).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll paste what I think the manifest should look like long term (rough):

```yaml
backupFormatVersion: 1.1.0
createdAt: 2025-02-06T19:05:12Z
platformVersion: 2025.02.1
components:
  controlPlaneDb:
    engine: postgres
    logicalDump: true
    artifact: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
  k8s:
    includedNamespaces: ["redwood-system", "redwood-control-plane"]
    artifact: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
crypto:
  envelope:
    provider: aws-kms
    keyRef: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:...:key/...
  dataKeyAlg: AES-256-GCM
integrity:
  manifestSha256: ...
  artifacts:
    - path: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
    - path: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
compat:
  minRestoreToolVersion: 0.9.0
  maxTestedRestoreToolVersion: 1.3.2
notes: "generated by redwood-private-installer"
```

Ethan Park: That’s good. `platformVersion` is important for data migrations. But I’m not sure about `maxTestedRestoreToolVersion` (feels weird). Maybe better: `createdByToolVersion` only, and our docs say what is supported.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. “max tested” will rot fast and look like a promise.

Miles Thornton: Maybe:
- `createdBy.tool` + `createdBy.version`
- `required.restoreTool.minVersion` (rare)
- and keep the rest as docs/compat matrix.

Allison Grant: For telemetry: include `backupId` UUID so we can correlate backup + restore drill + metrics. But for air-gapped, it’s still useful for logs.

Benji Okafor: Another detail: archive layout. Do we want deterministic layout?
`manifest.yaml` at root + `artifacts/` directory with fixed names. No timestamps in filenames.

Connor O'Brien: Please yes. Deterministic layout helps signing + reproducible bundles. Also in air-gapped import/export we want predictable paths.

Marcus Lin: Deterministic is good, but don’t bake secrets into path names (customer names, tenant ids). Keep it generic.

Michael Nguyen: Re: migration strategy: If we bump MAJOR, do we ship `backup migrate` that rewrites old to new?

Miles Thornton: Ideally yes, but only if necessary. Another model is: restore tool understands multiple majors (e.g., supports 1.x and 2.x). But that increases code path complexity.

Neha Kapoor: SRE preference: fewer migration steps during an incident. In DR you’re already stressed; “run migrate, then restore” adds risk.

Ethan Park: +1. If we can, keep restore tool able to read at least 1 previous major. But we can reserve the right not to.

Connor O'Brien: Release reality: maintaining multiple majors indefinitely is expensive. But supporting at least “current major and previous major” seems doable.

Marcus Lin: Security preference: fewer legacy parsers forever. Each parser is an attack surface.

Hanae Suzuki: If we do multi-major, enforce strict parsing + size limits (zip bombs etc). Also signature verification BEFORE extraction.

Benji Okafor: +1 to “verify before extract.” Current PR-512 does checksum verification but we should ensure we’re not untarring untrusted content prior to verifying.

Michael Nguyen: So we’re converging on:
- Semver `backupFormatVersion`
- Restore supports: current MAJOR and optionally previous MAJOR (policy)
- New restore tool must restore old backups within same MAJOR (all MINOR/PATCH)
- MAJOR bumps are rare + ADR required

Miles Thornton: Yes. Also add: MINOR changes must be additive + optional. PATCH changes must not alter semantics.

Ethan Park: Another tricky bit: DB dumps. If we do logical dumps, those are not always forward/back compat across postgres versions. But that’s not “backup format,” that’s “component artifact.” We need to document what postgres versions are supported.

Neha Kapoor: Put it in the RPO/RTO + supported versions matrix. But on restore, tool should preflight check the target environment matches requirements (db engine/version).

Allison Grant: And emit a metric/event when preflight fails due to version mismatch so we can see it.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Miles/Michael: update ADR-027 addendum or new ADR for semver policy + multi-major support.
- Connor: add release gate: backup format changes require bump + changelog snippet.
- Benji: ensure installer checks `backupFormatVersion` range and errors with pointer to docs.

Marcus Lin: Add to ADR: “unknown major => fail closed.” And explicitly state we won’t ever auto-downgrade or “best effort” restore.

Hanae Suzuki: Also include statement about key rotation and what happens if KMS unavailable during restore (fail, don’t fallback to plaintext).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll comment on ENG-9830 with this proposal and link it to PR-512 notes.

Miles Thornton: Cool. I’ll draft the ADR text tonight and tag folks for review.

Michael Nguyen: On restore we can do: allow unknown keys in manifest but require known `backupFormatVersion` range. Unknown keys get logged (debug). If MAJOR mismatched, error. If MINOR higher than supported, error unless we can safely ignore.

Ethan Park: That turns into: restore supports `MAJOR == X` and `MINOR <= maxMinorSupported`. That’s fine.

Connor O'Brien: But you’re describing “new backup might not restore on older installer,” which is ok. We care more about “new installer restoring old backup.”

Neha Kapoor: Exactly. Customer expectation: you can upgrade tooling and still restore the last few backups.

Miles Thornton: So compatibility direction: backward compat for restore (new tool restores old backups). Forward compat (old tool restores new backup) not required.

Hanae Suzuki: With encryption: if we change encryption envelope format, that’s a MAJOR. But we should isolate encryption metadata in manifest and keep ciphertext opaque. Ideally: encryption is “provider + parameters” and doesn’t change often.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also: manifest should include KMS key reference (arn/uri) but never include plaintext material. And we should not back up actual secret values if we can avoid it; references only (aligns w/ ADR-027).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll paste what I think the manifest should look like long term (rough):

```yaml
backupFormatVersion: 1.1.0
createdAt: 2025-02-06T19:05:12Z
platformVersion: 2025.02.1
components:
  controlPlaneDb:
    engine: postgres
    logicalDump: true
    artifact: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
  k8s:
    includedNamespaces: ["redwood-system", "redwood-control-plane"]
    artifact: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
crypto:
  envelope:
    provider: aws-kms
    keyRef: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:...:key/...
  dataKeyAlg: AES-256-GCM
integrity:
  manifestSha256: ...
  artifacts:
    - path: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
    - path: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
compat:
  minRestoreToolVersion: 0.9.0
  maxTestedRestoreToolVersion: 1.3.2
notes: "generated by redwood-private-installer"
```

Ethan Park: That’s good. `platformVersion` is important for data migrations. But I’m not sure about `maxTestedRestoreToolVersion` (feels weird). Maybe better: `createdByToolVersion` only, and our docs say what is supported.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. “max tested” will rot fast and look like a promise.

Miles Thornton: Maybe:
- `createdBy.tool` + `createdBy.version`
- `required.restoreTool.minVersion` (rare)
- and keep the rest as docs/compat matrix.

Allison Grant: For telemetry: include `backupId` UUID so we can correlate backup + restore drill + metrics. But for air-gapped, it’s still useful for logs.

Benji Okafor: Another detail: archive layout. Do we want deterministic layout?
`manifest.yaml` at root + `artifacts/` directory with fixed names. No timestamps in filenames.

Connor O'Brien: Please yes. Deterministic layout helps signing + reproducible bundles. Also in air-gapped import/export we want predictable paths.

Marcus Lin: Deterministic is good, but don’t bake secrets into path names (customer names, tenant ids). Keep it generic.

Michael Nguyen: Re: migration strategy: If we bump MAJOR, do we ship `backup migrate` that rewrites old to new?

Miles Thornton: Ideally yes, but only if necessary. Another model is: restore tool understands multiple majors (e.g., supports 1.x and 2.x). But that increases code path complexity.

Neha Kapoor: SRE preference: fewer migration steps during an incident. In DR you’re already stressed; “run migrate, then restore” adds risk.

Ethan Park: +1. If we can, keep restore tool able to read at least 1 previous major. But we can reserve the right not to.

Connor O'Brien: Release reality: maintaining multiple majors indefinitely is expensive. But supporting at least “current major and previous major” seems doable.

Marcus Lin: Security preference: fewer legacy parsers forever. Each parser is an attack surface.

Hanae Suzuki: If we do multi-major, enforce strict parsing + size limits (zip bombs etc). Also signature verification BEFORE extraction.

Benji Okafor: +1 to “verify before extract.” Current PR-512 does checksum verification but we should ensure we’re not untarring untrusted content prior to verifying.

Michael Nguyen: So we’re converging on:
- Semver `backupFormatVersion`
- Restore supports: current MAJOR and optionally previous MAJOR (policy)
- New restore tool must restore old backups within same MAJOR (all MINOR/PATCH)
- MAJOR bumps are rare + ADR required

Miles Thornton: Yes. Also add: MINOR changes must be additive + optional. PATCH changes must not alter semantics.

Ethan Park: Another tricky bit: DB dumps. If we do logical dumps, those are not always forward/back compat across postgres versions. But that’s not “backup format,” that’s “component artifact.” We need to document what postgres versions are supported.

Neha Kapoor: Put it in the RPO/RTO + supported versions matrix. But on restore, tool should preflight check the target environment matches requirements (db engine/version).

Allison Grant: And emit a metric/event when preflight fails due to version mismatch so we can see it.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Miles/Michael: update ADR-027 addendum or new ADR for semver policy + multi-major support.
- Connor: add release gate: backup format changes require bump + changelog snippet.
- Benji: ensure installer checks `backupFormatVersion` range and errors with pointer to docs.

Marcus Lin: Add to ADR: “unknown major => fail closed.” And explicitly state we won’t ever auto-downgrade or “best effort” restore.

Hanae Suzuki: Also include statement about key rotation and what happens if KMS unavailable during restore (fail, don’t fallback to plaintext).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll comment on ENG-9830 with this proposal and link it to PR-512 notes.

Miles Thornton: Cool. I’ll draft the ADR text tonight and tag folks for review.

title: #eng-infra: 1738791123
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: trying to lock baseline metrics for “time-to-ready” before we build the new state machine. need alignment on: what exact timestamp = “allocated” and what exact timestamp = “ready”. also: what’s the source of truth so we don’t have 3 different numbers floating around.

Miles Thornton: +1. for SLI we need start/end events that are (a) consistent across regions/GPU types, (b) collectible today (even if messy), and (c) same semantics we’ll use post-sprint.

Aisha Bello: if we can’t get the canonical events yet, I’d rather define the SLI now and do “best-effort backfill” than pick something like terraform apply time.

Keiko Yamamoto: please not terraform apply time 🙃 that’s gonna include human delays + CI queueing + retries. also apply can be rerun.

Jamal Pierce: from capacity ops POV, “allocated” is basically when we see instance IDs created + tagged to the pool. but depending on cloud, the instance can be “created” and still stuck in pending for a while.

Vanessa Ortiz: right. my original thought was:
- start = EC2 RunInstances response time OR when instance appears in fleet inventory
- end = node is Ready in k8s
but “Ready in k8s” != “ready for inference” (driver mismatch, etc).

Rafael Mendes: for baseline (pre-gates), “ready for inference” is fuzzy. but we can split into two:
1) infra-ready: kubelet joined + NodeReady true
2) serving-ready: node added to serving pool / schedulable for inference

Harish Reddy: current manual checklist basically ends when the node shows up in the pool and can take a canary request. we do sometimes mark nodes schedulable and then find out drivers are off.

Benji Okafor: from control plane side, we *don’t* currently have a single “serving-ready” event persisted. we have:
- cluster join (from kube api)
- pool membership changes (from fleet tooling)
- some logs in provisioning orchestrator

Jordan Lee: the ADR we’re drafting uses Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready. for baseline, we can define Allocated = “instance created + recorded in fleet inventory” and Ready = “node admitted to serving pool” (or “marked ready” in fleet tool).

Ethan Park: question: do we have a DB field today for “added to serving pool”? if not, baseline might need to use kube node label/taint changes as a proxy.

Allison Grant: reminder: whatever we pick needs to be doable as an SLI later (events or metrics). also please avoid per-node high-cardinality tags in the interim dashboards.

Sergio Costa: we can compute baseline from logs if we standardize a correlation key (instance_id / node_name). but if start is cloudtrail and end is kube, it’s gonna be brittle.

Vanessa Ortiz: what do we have *reliably* right now?
- EC2 instance LaunchTime
- our fleet inventory service writes a record when it discovers the instance (poller)
- kube node creation time
- kube NodeReady condition lastTransitionTime
- pool assignment is via labels like `redwood.ai/pool-id` (I think?)

Keiko Yamamoto: the poller timestamp (fleet inventory record created_at) is probably closer to “allocated” in our system than EC2 LaunchTime. LaunchTime requires AWS API + permissions and can differ by provider.

Miles Thornton: I’d prefer start = “we know about it” (inventory created_at), since that reflects our operational latency too (discovery). BUT the sprint goal is “provision-to-ready” which should start at allocation (customer-visible lead time). discovery delay might be part of that? depends on messaging.

Jamal Pierce: for Dedicated, customers feel it from the moment we accept the capacity expansion request. but we don’t have a single timestamp for that either.

Talia Benson: for sprint reporting, Irene/Neha asked for “time from capacity allocated to ready for workloads” (their words). i’d avoid “request accepted” because that’s more GTM/ops process.

Aisha Bello: can we standardize start = “cloud instance created” where available, and fall back to inventory created_at where not? (and document the fallback)

Rafael Mendes: I’d rather pick one canonical definition even if imperfect. if we do multi-source fallbacks, we’ll spend the whole sprint arguing about deltas.

Allison Grant: +1. pick one now, then in the new system emit a dedicated “allocated_at” event from the orchestrator.

Benji Okafor: proposal for baseline:
- allocated_ts = fleet inventory record created_at (when instance_id appears + has pool tags)
- ready_ts = first time kube node is NodeReady=true AND has pool label AND is not tainted `NoSchedule` (or whatever we use)
this approximates “eligible to serve”.

Harish Reddy: caveat: we sometimes remove NoSchedule before drivers fully verified. but baseline will reflect reality today (including that footgun).

Keiko Yamamoto: on labels/taints: for Dedicated pools we do:
- label `redwood.ai/dedicated-pool=<pool_id>`
- taint `redwood.ai/provisioning=true:NoSchedule` until we flip it
at least in us-east.

Sergio Costa: if that’s consistent, we can compute ready_ts from kube events. also: NodeReady condition can flap; use first time it becomes Ready *and stays* for N minutes?

Miles Thornton: for SLI, we usually define end as “entered Ready state” (first transition), not “stayed ready”. but we can add a “stability” metric later.

Rafael Mendes: for baseline, keep it simple: first transition to Ready + taint removed. if it flaps later, that’s a different SLI.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok, so sounds like:
START (“allocated”): fleet inventory created_at for instance_id with pool tags
END (“ready”): kube node has NodeReady=true AND provisioning taint removed AND pool label present
source of truth for baseline: derived query (inventory DB + kube API)

Jordan Lee: + document that this is baseline only. post-sprint, source of truth becomes Fleet Readiness Service state transitions (Allocated->Ready) persisted in control plane.

Ethan Park: works for me. we should add a note that kube timestamps are from api-server; inventory timestamps from our DB. clock skew risk is low but non-zero.

Aisha Bello: I can draft the metric definition as:
`time_to_ready_seconds = ready_ts - allocated_ts`
Dimensions: region, gpu_type, pool_id (maybe pool_id only in logs, not in metrics)

Allison Grant: please no pool_id as a metric label. region + gpu_type + step is ok. pool_id can be a filter in logs/traces.

Sergio Costa: +1. we can keep pool_id in the spreadsheet/backfill job output, but dashboard metrics should aggregate.

Jamal Pierce: can we also capture P50/P95 by GPU type (A100/H100/L4 etc) and by region? that’s what capacity wants.

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. i’ll update the baseline sheet with these definitions + note gaps. also adding action item: Keiko + Sergio to confirm taint/label names in each region.

Keiko Yamamoto: i’ll sanity check labels/taints in us-east + eu-west today and report back.

Sergio Costa: I’ll put together a quick query/script outline. if kube event scraping is gross, we can do it via the kube API server and dump timestamps.

Rafael Mendes: once we have baseline numbers, SRE can propose initial SLO thresholds (even if we don’t page on them yet).

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. locking this unless strong objections. :thumbsup:

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: (and +1 on documenting that “ready” here is “schedulable” not “passed validations”).

Allison Grant: please paste the exact taint key + label keys into the doc once confirmed. saves us a lot of drift later.

title: #architecture: 1738703321
preview:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.

title: #eng-infra: 1738441123
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: Kicking off sizing + initial rollout seq for Dedicated burst pools. Need alignment w/ Product on assumptions we can safely commit to (and what we should *not* promise). I’m thinking we start w/ 1 region + 1 hardware class (us-east H100) for beta. Thoughts?

Lauren Bishop: +1. For PRD we need an “initial coverage” statement for beta/GA. Sales is already asking “which regions first” (ofc). What’s feasible given procurement + safety margins?

Jamal Pierce: From capacity planning: we can stand up a small managed burst pool in us-east H100 without risking baseline Dedicated supply. us-west H100 is tighter until late Q2. eu-west L40S is easier sooner but demand is less predictable.

Harish Reddy: Operationally we can only run game day + oncall playbooks in a limited set first. Preference: us-east H100 → us-west H100 → eu-west L40S. (Keeping hardware classes minimal early reduces incident surface.)

Keiko Yamamoto: On guardrails side, I want to anchor on a few hard numbers so we’re not hand-wavy:
- Per-tenant max burst ratio: propose 1.5x baseline GPUs (e.g., 10 → up to 15) initially.
- Cooldown window after hitting cap: 10 min before we allow re-admit above baseline (prevents sawtooth thrash).
- Pool-level “fleet safety” cap: keep pool allocation <= 20% of that region/hw’s non-reserved headroom.
We can tune later but need safe defaults.

Lauren Bishop: If we do 1.5x baseline, can we still satisfy “weekly indexing jobs” use case? A few customers asked for 2x+ during a 1–2 hour window.

Vanessa Ortiz: 2x is where we start impacting neighbor risk if multiple tenants burst simultaneously. Unless pool is larger. But bigger pool means more slack inventory (or we steal from future Dedicated growth). We can position higher ratios as “by exception” later w/ priority tiers.

Rishi Malhotra: From margin model: burst should be priced to discourage “permanent burst as baseline.” If we allow 2x broadly, we’ll get customers treating it as elastic dedicated. That breaks unit econ unless pool is huge and utilization low. Strong preference to start 1.25–1.5x, measure, then expand.

Lauren Bishop: Got it. For GA messaging we can say “up to 1.5x baseline subject to pool availability + guardrails.” But we need a consistent region list.

Ethan Park: Control plane wise, region/hw allowlist is easy. We can have flags like `burst_enabled_regions=[us-east]` and `burst_enabled_hw=[H100]` per tenant + global gate. Just need the planned sequence so we don’t churn configs.

Vanessa Ortiz: Proposed rollout sequence (beta → GA):
1) us-east / H100
2) us-west / H100
3) eu-west / L40S
4) us-east / L40S (if needed)
5) eu-central / H100 (only if supply stabilizes)
Reasoning: H100 in us-east is most demanded and we have the most telemetry maturity there.

Jamal Pierce: +1, but note: eu-west H100 lead times are volatile. If we do EU early, L40S is the safe bet.

Keiko Yamamoto: Also sizing assumptions I want written down (so everyone stops asking in DMs):
- Target steady-state utilization of burst pool: 35–50% (we need headroom for spikes)
- Safety buffer: 30% of pool kept unallocatable via circuit breaker when tail latency deltas exceed threshold
- Telemetry lag assumption: up to 15s delayed utilization; admission controller must be conservative above ~80% reported saturation

Lauren Bishop: Can we translate that into “pool size” guidance? Like % of total Dedicated baseline in region?

Vanessa Ortiz: Roughly yes. Initial pass:
- Pool size per region/hw = 10–15% of *active Dedicated baseline GPUs* in that region/hw, with a hard minimum (so we’re not useless). Example: if us-east H100 Dedicated baseline is 800 GPUs, burst pool starts 80–120 GPUs.
This assumes not all tenants burst simultaneously and admission fairness does its job.

Rishi Malhotra: Finance is ok with 10–15% pool sizing if we enforce:
- per-tenant cap (1.5x)
- global cap (pool cannot exceed X% of headroom)
- metering is accurate (no double counting on retries)

Harish Reddy: + ops note: if pool is < ~50 GPUs, it’s noisy and hard to reason about. Denials spike and CS gets hammered. I like having a minimum of 64 GPUs for H100 pools where possible.

Vanessa Ortiz: Agree. We can set min pool size thresholds by hw class:
- H100: min 64 GPUs
- L40S: min 128 GPUs (cheaper, but need more units for meaningful throughput)
We’ll validate w/ Jamal.

Jamal Pierce: Those mins seem fine; for L40S, power/rack constraints in eu-west might cap initial pool at ~96–128 GPUs anyway.

Lauren Bishop: For PRD I’ll incorporate:
- initial rollout order (us-east H100 first)
- initial burst ratio (up to 1.5x)
- “subject to pool availability; not guaranteed capacity” language
But Sales will ask: “When us-west?”

Vanessa Ortiz: Tentative dates (not commitments):
- us-east H100: beta in May (dogfood earlier)
- us-west H100: GA target June-ish pending supply
- eu-west L40S: after GA, staggered
We should keep it “staged rollout by region/hw based on fleet availability + SLO gates.”

CapacityBot: Reminder: please update the fleet capacity tracker with new assumptions. Template: https://redwood-inference.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/ENGINFRA/pages/Capacity+Planning+Templates (internal)

Keiko Yamamoto: One more thing: procurement risk by region should change safety buffer. For us-west H100, I want +10% extra buffer until supply stabilizes. So pool sizing might be 8–12% there vs 10–15% in us-east.

Jamal Pierce: Makes sense. I’ll add a “region risk multiplier” to the sizing model:
- us-east: 1.0
- us-west: 0.8
- eu-west: 0.9
This affects recommended pool size and the circuit breaker trigger points.

Lauren Bishop: Perfect. Can we summarize “decision” + “open questions” so I can paste to the PRD + the launch plan ticket?

Vanessa Ortiz: Summary:
DECISIONS
- Initial rollout sequence: us-east/H100 → us-west/H100 → eu-west/L40S.
- Initial per-tenant max burst ratio: 1.5x baseline (default), higher only later/exception.
- Pool sizing starting point: 10–15% of active Dedicated baseline GPUs in region/hw, with hw minimums (H100 min 64, L40S min 128) and region risk multipliers.
- Keep explicit safety buffers + global circuit breaker tied to neighbor latency indicators.
OPEN QUESTIONS
- Final numbers for cooldown window + denial mode (queue vs immediate) need SRE signoff.
- Whether we need eu-central in 2025H2 depends on supply.
ACTION ITEMS
- Jamal: update sizing model w/ region risk multipliers + mins.
- Lauren: update PRD rollout section + messaging.
- Keiko: propose concrete circuit breaker thresholds for pool saturation + tail latency deltas.
- Harish: confirm ops readiness checklist per region/hw for go/no-go.

Lauren Bishop: :thumbsup: Works. I’ll sync with Sales enablement that us-west is “staged” not a fixed date.

Harish Reddy: I’ll start a runbook stub for “pool too small → denial storm” and when to temporarily raise pool size vs tighten caps.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll drop proposed thresholds in this thread once I have numbers. (Need to look at tail amplification data from last quarter’s H100 fleet.)

Vanessa Ortiz: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread updated as model changes; I’ll also link the burst-pool-sizing-policy doc once it’s in draft.

title: #eng: 1738266018
preview:
Kira Thompson: Dropping the draft Confluence runbook here for review before we socialize w/ Support + SE. Please be brutal 🙏 https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PRIV/Control+Plane+Backup+Runbook+(Draft)

Elliot Price: Skimmed. Overall good structure, but there are a few spots that will confuse customers.
- Step 0 “Prereqs” is too light. We need explicit versions + permissions.
- The “quiesce” section reads like it’s optional, but later we assume we did it.
- Validation section is mostly “check pods are running” — we need functional checks.

Sean Gallagher: +1. From SRE view, I can’t tell what a successful backup *looks like*.
Runbook says “verify artifact integrity” but doesn’t show an actual command/output or what to do if it fails.
Also missing paging/alerting tie-in (even if just “see dashboard X”).

Kira Thompson: Noted. Can you call out the exact missing prereqs you want listed?

Sean Gallagher: Minimum:
- `kubectl` access w/ cluster-admin OR specific RBAC (and list the verbs/resources)
- storage backend configured (S3/NFS) and tested write/read
- KMS configured (if using envelope) and “kms outage behavior” statement
- time sync check (we hit clock skew in staging once)
- “do you need Velero installed?” (we say “optional” but then reference it later)

Ethan Park: On the quiesce bit: for control plane we *do* need to pause reconcilers during snapshot or you can get partially applied state. Draft currently says “if needed” which is too vague.
We should specify:
1) scale down `redwood-controller-manager` (or set `--reconcile=false` if we support that)
2) confirm no migrations running
3) then snapshot DB
4) then resume

Benji Okafor: Agree. Also the runbook says “snapshot config DB” but doesn’t name it. For Private we’ve got `rw-config-postgres` (statefulset) in most installs, but some customers run external Postgres.
We should split into “managed in-cluster DB” vs “external DB” branches and put a giant warning that we don’t handle external DB snapshotting beyond “use your DB vendor snapshot”.

Kira Thompson: Good point. I can add a decision tree.

Hanae Suzuki: Security gaps:
- We say “encrypted at rest” but don’t say how the DEK is generated / where KEK lives (esp for customer-managed keys).
- Need explicit statement: backups must NOT contain plaintext secret material. We only store *references* / metadata, and the restore expects secrets to be provisioned via K8s secret store / external KMS.
- If we *do* include any K8s Secrets for bootstrap (I saw a line implying that), we should be super explicit and likely remove it.

Elliot Price: +1. The draft currently says “export secrets” in one step (under “collect k8s resources”). That’ll set off alarms.
Maybe reword as “export secret references + verify secrets exist in target cluster”.

Bruno Silva: Also the “collect k8s resources” section is confusing. Are we backing up:
A) Redwood control plane state only (DB + object store manifests)
B) full cluster resources (Velero)
Right now it’s kind of both.
My suggestion: keep the primary path as “Redwood backup artifact” via installer CLI, and make Velero an appendix for cluster-level stuff.

Talia Benson: From program POV: we should align with the ADR language. If the decision is “manifest + encrypted tarball w/ versioned schema”, the runbook should mirror those terms.
If there’s a dependency on PR-512 compatibility checks, call it out.

Connor O'Brien: Release/packaging nit: the runbook uses `latest` in a couple examples (`curl .../installer/latest`). For on-prem / regulated, we must pin versions.
Also add a note: use the *same* installer version for restore as used for backup (or newer only if compatible). And point to compatibility matrix once it exists.

Kira Thompson: Re “successful backup looks like” — do we want to include sample `backup create` output?

Benji Okafor: Yes. Something like:
- command: `rw-private backup create --output s3://... --kms-key ...`
- expected: “manifest.json written”, “artifact.enc size > 0”, “sha256 verified”, “backup_id=...”, “format_version=v1”
- then a “where to find logs” section.

Sean Gallagher: And for restore, include an explicit “abort criteria”. Example:
- if post-restore health checks fail for >15 min
- if migrations auto-start unexpectedly
- if routing policies differ from manifest
Right now it’s “rollback if needed” which is too hand-wavy.

Ethan Park: Validation gaps after restore:
We need at least:
- hit `/readyz` and `/healthz` for control-plane services (or whatever endpoints we have)
- run “tenant list” / “models list” from the control plane API and compare counts
- verify routing policy objects restored (the ones that pin model versions)
- generate a small inference request end-to-end (if data plane is up)

Markus Klein: Adding SE/customer POV: the draft assumes they know what “tenant config” includes. We should spell out examples:
- org/tenant IDs
- API keys? (probably not backed up)
- model pins / routing rules
- quotas
Also a FAQ: “Will my audit logs be restored?” (answer probably “export separately”).

Hanae Suzuki: Re API keys: please don’t imply we back up customer secrets. If keys are stored in DB, we need a clear story. If not, then runbook should say “re-provision credentials post-restore”.

Elliot Price: Another confusing thing: prereqs don’t mention CRDs. On restore, if CRDs aren’t installed yet, applying manifests fails (we saw that in SUP-18477).
Add a prerequisite: “install same Helm chart version / CRDs first, then restore state”.

Bruno Silva: +1. Maybe explicitly list bootstrap order:
1) install CRDs
2) install base chart (but keep reconcilers paused)
3) restore DB/object state
4) resume reconcilers
5) run validation

Sean Gallagher: Also: “where artifacts are stored” section should include retention + immutability guidance.
For S3: recommend bucket versioning + Object Lock if they want ransomware posture.
For NFS: at least mention snapshotting / read-only export pattern.

Connor O'Brien: Small doc clarity: use consistent names “backup artifact”, “backup bundle”, “snapshot”. Currently switches terms and it’s easy to misread.

Kira Thompson: This is super helpful. Action items I’m taking:
- Expand Prereqs (RBAC, storage, KMS, time sync, CRDs, pinned versions)
- Make quiesce mandatory + concrete steps
- Split in-cluster vs external DB
- Clarify scope: primary = Redwood artifact; Velero appendix
- Add sample CLI output + “what success looks like”
- Add explicit restore bootstrap order + abort criteria
- Beef up validation with functional/API checks + routing policy verification

Ethan Park: I’ll send you exact component names + the “pause reconcilers” approach we want to standardize on. We should avoid telling people to `kubectl scale` random deployments if we can provide a supported flag.

Sean Gallagher: If you add a “validation checklist” section, I can propose the minimal smoke tests we want on-call to run. We’ll want it to match what the automated restore drill runs.

Kira Thompson: Perfect. I’ll push v2 today and re-drop for final pass.

Talia Benson: From program POV: we should align with the ADR language. If the decision is “manifest + encrypted tarball w/ versioned schema”, the runbook should mirror those terms.
If there’s a dependency on PR-512 compatibility checks, call it out.

Connor O'Brien: Release/packaging nit: the runbook uses `latest` in a couple examples (`curl .../installer/latest`). For on-prem / regulated, we must pin versions.
Also add a note: use the *same* installer version for restore as used for backup (or newer only if compatible). And point to compatibility matrix once it exists.

Kira Thompson: Re “successful backup looks like” — do we want to include sample `backup create` output?

Benji Okafor: Yes. Something like:
- command: `rw-private backup create --output s3://... --kms-key ...`
- expected: “manifest.json written”, “artifact.enc size > 0”, “sha256 verified”, “backup_id=...”, “format_version=v1”
- then a “where to find logs” section.

Sean Gallagher: And for restore, include an explicit “abort criteria”. Example:
- if post-restore health checks fail for >15 min
- if migrations auto-start unexpectedly
- if routing policies differ from manifest
Right now it’s “rollback if needed” which is too hand-wavy.

Ethan Park: Validation gaps after restore:
We need at least:
- hit `/readyz` and `/healthz` for control-plane services (or whatever endpoints we have)
- run “tenant list” / “models list” from the control plane API and compare counts
- verify routing policy objects restored (the ones that pin model versions)
- generate a small inference request end-to-end (if data plane is up)

Markus Klein: Adding SE/customer POV: the draft assumes they know what “tenant config” includes. We should spell out examples:
- org/tenant IDs
- API keys? (probably not backed up)
- model pins / routing rules
- quotas
Also a FAQ: “Will my audit logs be restored?” (answer probably “export separately”).

Hanae Suzuki: Re API keys: please don’t imply we back up customer secrets. If keys are stored in DB, we need a clear story. If not, then runbook should say “re-provision credentials post-restore”.

Elliot Price: Another confusing thing: prereqs don’t mention CRDs. On restore, if CRDs aren’t installed yet, applying manifests fails (we saw that in SUP-18477).
Add a prerequisite: “install same Helm chart version / CRDs first, then restore state”.

Bruno Silva: +1. Maybe explicitly list bootstrap order:
1) install CRDs
2) install base chart (but keep reconcilers paused)
3) restore DB/object state
4) resume reconcilers
5) run validation

Sean Gallagher: Also: “where artifacts are stored” section should include retention + immutability guidance.
For S3: recommend bucket versioning + Object Lock if they want ransomware posture.
For NFS: at least mention snapshotting / read-only export pattern.

Connor O'Brien: Small doc clarity: use consistent names “backup artifact”, “backup bundle”, “snapshot”. Currently switches terms and it’s easy to misread.

Kira Thompson: This is super helpful. Action items I’m taking:
- Expand Prereqs (RBAC, storage, KMS, time sync, CRDs, pinned versions)
- Make quiesce mandatory + concrete steps
- Split in-cluster vs external DB
- Clarify scope: primary = Redwood artifact; Velero appendix
- Add sample CLI output + “what success looks like”
- Add explicit restore bootstrap order + abort criteria
- Beef up validation with functional/API checks + routing policy verification

Ethan Park: I’ll send you exact component names + the “pause reconcilers” approach we want to standardize on. We should avoid telling people to `kubectl scale` random deployments if we can provide a supported flag.

Sean Gallagher: If you add a “validation checklist” section, I can propose the minimal smoke tests we want on-call to run. We’ll want it to match what the automated restore drill runs.

Kira Thompson: Perfect. I’ll push v2 today and re-drop for final pass.

title: #eng-platform: 1738095544
preview:
Kevin Osei: Quick q on `schema_version` strategy for audit events + exporter normalization. For v1 rollout: do we require every producer to set `schema_version`, or can exporter backfill it when it recognizes a legacy shape?

Kevin Osei: Context: exporter is currently seeing a mix of shapes in the sink (some older control plane stuff still has `tenantId`, `action`, etc). Need something deterministic for SIEM customers.

Michael Nguyen: My take: producers MUST emit `schema_version` once they adopt v1. But exporter should also *normalize* legacy events and set `schema_version` on the *normalized* output.

Michael Nguyen: Otherwise customers get a split-brain stream forever.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Telemetry side also needs to segment by version. If `schema_version` is missing, dashboards become “maybe v1, maybe not” :grimacing:

Allison Grant: we should be explicit in the schema doc: `schema_version` is required field for v1 payloads.

Allison Grant: exporter behavior is a separate “compat layer” that outputs v1 even if input is legacy. (And should preserve raw fields somewhere if needed.)

Kevin Osei: Okay, but what do we call it when exporter is the one setting it? Do we need `schema_version` + `source_schema_version` or something?

Michael Nguyen: I’d avoid adding more fields unless we need it. But we can put the raw version (or “legacy”) in an extension namespace.

Michael Nguyen: e.g. `ext.exporter.raw_schema_version` or `ext.exporter.normalized_from`.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboards: we only care about the version of the event *after* normalization, because that’s what consumers see.

Logan Wright: But it’d be nice to slice “how much legacy is still flowing” for rollout tracking.

Diego Santos: Also consider Private: customers can lag on exporter upgrades. If we ship producers that start emitting v1, older exporter should pass it through without breaking.

Diego Santos: So: v1 events must be “additive / tolerant” on the exporter side. No strict reject unless explicitly configured.

Ethan Park: Agree. Exporter should validate *outgoing* payload against v1 schema but be lenient on input.

Ethan Park: In other words, exporter owns: (1) normalization, (2) tagging `schema_version`, (3) optional drop/alert on un-normalizable events.

Kevin Osei: That matches what I’m implementing in pr-842. Just want to confirm the exact tagging rules.

Kevin Osei: Here’s an example legacy event we still see:

```json
{
  "id": "7b1d9e74-acde-4e6a-9d10-9f9f2f8a9f2b",
  "time": "2025-01-29T21:18:02.901Z",
  "tenantId": "tnt_4h2k...",
  "action": "rbac.role.update",
  "actor": {"email": "admin@customer.com"},
  "resource": {"type": "role", "id": "role_123"},
  "ip": "203.0.113.4"
}
```

Kevin Osei: Proposed normalized output:

```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "7b1d9e74-acde-4e6a-9d10-9f9f2f8a9f2b",
  "event_time": "2025-01-29T21:18:02.901Z",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.updated",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_4h2k...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "email:admin@customer.com",
  "actor_display_name": "admin@customer.com",
  "source_ip": "203.0.113.4",
  "target_type": "rbac.role",
  "target_id": "role_123",
  "outcome": "success",
  "ext.exporter.normalized_from": "legacy"
}
```

Allison Grant: Looks reasonable. Minor nit: `event_type` should match taxonomy string exactly (we said past-tense verbs, right?). For RBAC updates I think we agreed on `rbac.role.updated` (so your normalized one is good) vs `rbac.role.update`.

Michael Nguyen: +1 and `event_time` should be RFC3339 with timezone (you have `Z`, so ok).

Nadia Rahman: for `actor_id`, prefixing like `email:` is fine as long as it’s consistent and we document it. Alternative is hashing, but then support/debug gets harder.

Allison Grant: we should avoid raw emails where possible, but audit logs often need it. If we keep email as display name, we should classify it (PII) + ensure retention guidance is clear.

Kevin Osei: yep, redaction doc will say: allow email in actor_display_name but don’t log arbitrary request payloads.

Ethan Park: Question: do we ever want `schema_version: "legacy"`?

Michael Nguyen: I’d avoid non-numeric schema versions. Some SIEMs assume sortable semantic-ish versions.

Michael Nguyen: Better: missing => exporter sets `schema_version: "1.0"` (because output is v1), and store `ext.exporter.input_shape: "legacy"`.

Logan Wright: +1. If schema_version means “shape of this event”, then after normalization it’s v1.

Diego Santos: Another corner: what about events that already have `schema_version` but are wrong (e.g., someone ships `"v1"`)?

Kevin Osei: I can implement: if producer provided `schema_version` and it doesn’t match `^\d+\.\d+$`, exporter rewrites to `1.0` + adds `ext.exporter.schema_version_rewritten=true` and increments a metric.

Allison Grant: That’s good. Prefer metrics + alert over hard dropping.

Ethan Park: +1 best-effort; don’t break admin paths.

Marcus Lin: Aligning on decision:
- `schema_version` is required for v1 producers (engineering standard)
- Exporter normalizes legacy -> emits v1 output with `schema_version: "1.0"`
- Exporter preserves provenance in `ext.exporter.*` (input_shape / normalized_from / rewrite flags)
- No `schema_version: "legacy"` in outbound payloads
- Exporter should be tolerant on input, strict(er) on output + emit metrics for anything it has to rewrite / can’t normalize

Marcus Lin: Kevin, can you add a short section to exporter README + link to schema versioning policy doc when it’s ready? :thumbsup:

Kevin Osei: yep will do. I’ll also add a couple unit tests for the `tenantId` -> `tenant_id` and `action` -> `event_type` mapping.

Logan Wright: If you can emit a counter like `audit_exporter_normalization_total{reason="legacy_missing_schema_version"}`, I can wire it into rollout dashboards.

Kevin Osei: done. Will add `reason` labels for: missing_schema_version, invalid_schema_version, legacy_field_map, unknown_event_type.

Allison Grant: last thing: for unknown event types, don’t invent. Map if known, else keep `event_type: "unknown"` + `ext.exporter.raw_action` and page the owner.

Michael Nguyen: +1. Also make sure we preserve `event_id` always. If legacy has no id, exporter should generate one (ULID?) + set `ext.exporter.generated_event_id=true`.

Kevin Osei: ack. We already discussed ULID for ordering; I’ll use ULID and add that flag.

Diego Santos: can we document the “heuristics” explicitly? Customers will ask how we decide something is legacy vs v1.

Kevin Osei: I’ll add a short table:
- if `schema_version` present and parseable => treat as v1-ish
- else if `tenantId` or `action` present => legacy normalization path
- else => unknown, best-effort map + metric

Marcus Lin: Sounds good. Let’s keep it simple and measurable.

title: #eng: 1737412205
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit requirements thread. Need to land on (1) RPO/RTO targets we’ll *commit* to by archetype, (2) what state is in-scope vs out, (3) who owns customer-facing docs vs internal runbooks. Please reply w/ what you’re hearing from customers + any “must include” components.

Vivek Kulkarni: From customer calls last 2 weeks: they don’t care about “backup” as a feature; they care about “I can recover and pass audit.” RPO/RTO asks are all over the place but recurring:
- VPC single-region: RPO 1h–4h, RTO 2h–8h (depends on whether we’re restoring into fresh cluster vs in-place)
- On-prem: RPO 24h is acceptable for some, but they want deterministic restore steps and evidence.
Also: they expect backups encrypted w/ customer-managed keys where available.

Ethan Park: Component inventory (control plane) that I think needs to be explicitly covered:
- Config DB (tenant config, routing policies, model pins, quotas)
- Secrets *references* (NOT secret material) – we should restore pointers/IDs, not values, esp if using KMS/HSM
- Object store metadata (if any) re: model artifacts cached? but probably out-of-scope for Day-2 unless we’re storing customer artifacts
- K8s manifests/Helm values for Redwood services (needed for deterministic redeploy)
We should be clear: data plane is stateless and should be redeployable from desired state.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on clarity. SRE perspective: if we ship this, on-call will get paged when it fails. So we need to define:
- what “success” means (backup integrity + restorable)
- expected cadence (daily? hourly?)
- how to validate (smoke test + periodic restore drill in staging)
RTO targets must include “restore validation time,” not just “we ran a command.”

Neha Kapoor: Agree. We should treat this as reliability surface area. I’d like SLO-ish definitions:
- Backup job success rate over 30d
- Restore drill success rate + p95 restore drill duration
We don’t need to over-promise for GA, but we need to publish what we *support*.

Marcus Lin: Security constraints to bake in early:
- Backups encrypted at rest by default (envelope encryption). Customer-managed keys when possible; document fallback behavior if KMS down.
- No plaintext secrets in backup artifacts. At most, store secret *names/paths* and require re-seeding secrets as a step.
- Audit logs: need retention guidance; some customers will require exporting audit logs to their SIEM / immutable store. If we touch audit logs, we should coordinate w/ Kevin.
Also: please define threat model assumptions for on-prem/air-gapped.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging angle (on-prem): if air-gapped, we need a signed/offline bundle for whatever CLI/scripts we ship. Also version compatibility: backup format version + restore checks.
If we want this in the installer repo, we need:
- deterministic artifact layout
- checksum manifest
- signature verification step (CI + runtime)

Elliot Price: On docs ownership: propose
- Customer-facing: docs site (Elliot owner, Kira writer)
- Internal/SRE: Confluence runbooks (Sean/Kira)
But we need input from Engineering on the exact steps and “known footguns.”

Kira Thompson: Works for me. If we can agree on in-scope components + prerequisites, I’ll start a runbook outline this week.
Question: are we including Kubernetes-level backups (Velero) or explicitly *not* and only doing app-level snapshots?

Bruno Silva: From Helm/Terraform side: customers are already doing Velero/etc. I think we should support two layers:
1) Redwood control-plane state backup (DB snapshot + config export + manifest)
2) Optional: guidance for k8s resources (Velero include/exclude list)
But we should avoid “Velero required” as a hard dep.

Miles Thornton: DR architecture take: don’t conflate “cluster recovery” w/ “app restore.”
- For etcd snapshot restore: infra playbook. Use only for catastrophic cluster corruption.
- For “restore into clean cluster”: our primary supported story.
RTO should be measured for the supported story (clean cluster), not worst-case etcd surgery.

Hanae Suzuki: On KMS/HSM: some private cloud customers have KMS-like APIs but not AWS/GCP. If we do a provider interface, we can support at least:
- AWS KMS
- GCP KMS (maybe later)
- on-prem HSM via PKCS#11? (might be too heavy for v1)
We should document key rotation expectations and what happens if decrypt fails.

Talia Benson: Super helpful. Trying to converge on a first pass:
In-scope (v1):
- Control plane config DB snapshot + restore
- Export of install config/Helm values + required k8s manifests to deterministically redeploy
- Secrets: references only; explicit step to re-provision secrets (w/ validation checks)
- Backup artifact format + encryption + integrity verification
- Restore validation (smoke tests)
Out-of-scope (v1):
- Data plane token/KV cache state
- Full cluster recovery (etcd restore) beyond a pointer to infra runbook
- Customer application data

- Control plane API health
- tenant config read/write
- routing policy present
- ability to issue a no-op inference request (or at least hit model registry endpoint)
- audit log pipeline healthy (if enabled)

Talia Benson: Kicking off the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit requirements thread. Need to land on (1) RPO/RTO targets we’ll *commit* to by archetype, (2) what state is in-scope vs out, (3) who owns customer-facing docs vs internal runbooks. Please reply w/ what you’re hearing from customers + any “must include” components.

Vivek Kulkarni: From customer calls last 2 weeks: they don’t care about “backup” as a feature; they care about “I can recover and pass audit.” RPO/RTO asks are all over the place but recurring:
- VPC single-region: RPO 1h–4h, RTO 2h–8h (depends on whether we’re restoring into fresh cluster vs in-place)
- On-prem: RPO 24h is acceptable for some, but they want deterministic restore steps and evidence.
Also: they expect backups encrypted w/ customer-managed keys where available.

Ethan Park: Component inventory (control plane) that I think needs to be explicitly covered:
- Config DB (tenant config, routing policies, model pins, quotas)
- Secrets *references* (NOT secret material) – we should restore pointers/IDs, not values, esp if using KMS/HSM
- Object store metadata (if any) re: model artifacts cached? but probably out-of-scope for Day-2 unless we’re storing customer artifacts
- K8s manifests/Helm values for Redwood services (needed for deterministic redeploy)
We should be clear: data plane is stateless and should be redeployable from desired state.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on clarity. SRE perspective: if we ship this, on-call will get paged when it fails. So we need to define:
- what “success” means (backup integrity + restorable)
- expected cadence (daily? hourly?)
- how to validate (smoke test + periodic restore drill in staging)
RTO targets must include “restore validation time,” not just “we ran a command.”

- Backup job success rate over 30d
- Restore drill success rate + p95 restore drill duration
We don’t need to over-promise for GA, but we need to publish what we *support*.
