title: #architecture: 1737324412
preview:
Allison Grant: dropping the proposed audit event taxonomy naming conventions here for quick review. main goals: consistent, SIEM-friendly, low-cardinality. pls sanity check + call out footguns.

Allison Grant: current proposal (rough):
- `event_type`: lower_snake with dot segments: `<domain>.<entity>.<action>`
- action is verb in present tense (create/update/delete/rotate/enable/disable/export.configure/etc)
- no IDs in `event_type` ever
- avoid logging raw diffs; include controlled summaries

Jordan Lee: +1 on dot segments. just want to make sure we don’t end up with 12 variants of “update”. can we define a small verb set and stick to it?

Allison Grant: yep, verb set is intended to be enumerated. draft list is like: create|update|delete|enable|disable|rotate|revoke|login|logout|impersonate|configure|export|install|upgrade|rollback

Kevin Osei: from SIEM side, dot segments are fine. Splunk folks usually build `event_type="rbac.role.update"` queries. pls don’t use camelCase.

Logan Wright: main concern: people will want to stuff “details” into event_type (e.g. `policy.fallback.update.added_region_us-east-1`). should be hard no.

Allison Grant: hard no :slightly_smiling_face: details go into structured fields.

Ethan Park: question: do we want `rbac.role.update` or `rbac.role.updated`? i prefer present tense since it matches “action” field semantics.

Jordan Lee: present tense reads better in filters IMO. it’s an event record but the type is “what happened”. either way, pick one.

Marcus Lin: choose one and enforce. don’t bike shed for weeks :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: ok, present tense it is.

Nadia Rahman: also pls avoid `event_type` prefixes like `audit.` or `redwood.`. redundant and wastes bytes. if we need namespace, do it in `producer.service`.

Allison Grant: agreed. taxonomies should be deployment-agnostic. we have `deployment_mode` / `environment` fields separately.

Paula Mendes: can we talk cardinality? biggest offenders we’ve seen:
- `request_path`
- raw `user_agent`
- “diff” field with full JSON
- free-form `reason` / “note”

Logan Wright: +1. for coverage dashboards, if people put request_path we get insane dimension explosion.

Kevin Osei: on `user_agent`: some customers like it for investigations, but raw UA is too high cardinality. recommend normalized fields?

Allison Grant: proposed:
- keep `source.user_agent` optional but *normalized* (e.g., `"Chrome"`, `"curl"`, `"redwood-sdk"`), not the full string
- keep raw UA only in debug logs, not audit logs

Ethan Park: what about `source.ip`? that’s technically high cardinality but also necessary.

Allison Grant: `source.ip` is allowed (investigation-critical). we’ll keep it but treat it as “approved high-cardinality” and not index it in every internal metric.

Logan Wright: +1. in telemetry we can hash it or keep it out of labels. audit log itself can contain it.

Jordan Lee: for policy changes, people will want to include full before/after. can we define `change_summary` as a bounded structure?

Allison Grant: yep. suggestion:
- `change.action`: `"set"|"add"|"remove"` (enum)
- `change.fields`: allow-list of field names that changed (strings from enum)
- `change.before` / `change.after`: strings, capped length, must not contain secrets
- optional `change.diff_hash`: sha256 of canonicalized JSON if you really need integrity

Kevin Osei: love `diff_hash`. helps when exporter retries cause dupes and customer wants de-dupe / correlation.

Marcus Lin: careful: `before/after` values can leak PII if it’s an email, etc. make sure we have a classification table + redaction guidance.

Allison Grant: totally. we’re mapping every field to classification. and `before/after` is only for config-ish enums, not for user-provided text.

Nadia Rahman: concrete example might help service teams.

Allison Grant: example draft:
```
event_type=rbac.role.update
actor.type=user
actor.id=usr_...
actor.email_hash=sha256(...)
resource.type=rbac_role
resource.id=role_...
change.fields=["permissions","name"]
change.before="permissions:viewer"
change.after="permissions:admin"
```

Ethan Park: nit: `resource.type=rbac_role` vs `rbac.role`? want consistent shape.

Jordan Lee: i’d vote `resource.type` as enum-ish tokens like `rbac_role`, `api_key`, `routing_policy`. then `event_type` stays dotted.

Allison Grant: yep, that’s where we landed in the schema draft. `resource.type` is an enum registry.

Logan Wright: pls also ban `resource.name` unless it’s constrained. names are often user-provided.

Allison Grant: we can allow `resource.display_name` only if it’s a system-generated slug and capped (e.g. max 64 chars) + character allow-list.

Kevin Osei: for exports, customers sometimes want “project name”. if we can’t include it, ok, but at least include stable IDs so they can join on their side.

Allison Grant: yep: IDs always, names optional w/ constraints.

Paula Mendes: also: please don’t log full prompt text / model output in audit logs (i know it sounds obvious but we’ve seen it in the wild).

Marcus Lin: +1000. audit logs are not app logs.

Jordan Lee: for routing policy events, can we standardize naming like:
- `policy.routing.update`
- `policy.fallback.update`
- `policy.allowlist.update`
vs going deeper like `routing.policy.update`

Allison Grant: preference is domain-first by ownership surface:
- `routing_policy.update`
- `routing_policy.fallback.update`
BUT i’m worried that mixing underscores + dots might be confusing.

Logan Wright: keep it consistent: dots separate “levels”, underscores inside tokens. so `policy_engine.routing_policy.update` is too long. maybe `policy.routing.update` with resource.type to disambiguate.

Kevin Osei: from SIEM, shorter `event_type` is better. `policy.routing.update` is fine if resource.type says `routing_policy`.

Ethan Park: +1. event_type shouldn’t encode service internals. `policy.routing.update` is clear.

Allison Grant: ok, proposal update:
- use high-level domains: `rbac.*`, `credential.*`, `policy.*`, `export.*`, `retention.*`, `private_install.*`
- keep resource.type as the specific thing (`api_key`, `routing_policy`, `siem_destination`, `retention_policy`)

Jordan Lee: works. for private installer, do we really want `private_install.install`? that reads weird.

Allison Grant: maybe `private.install` / `private.upgrade` / `private.rollback`? consistent with domain = private.

Marcus Lin: ok with `private.*` as long as it’s explicitly for the Private product/installer actions.

Nadia Rahman: last request: can we document “do not include request_path” in schema examples + lints? people will do it by habit.

Allison Grant: yes. we’ll add lint rules:
- reject `request_path`
- reject any `*.raw` fields in audit events
- enforce length caps on free-text fields
- enforce `event_type` registry (no ad hoc strings)

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: if we do that + a small approved list of high-card fields (source.ip, trace_id, request_id), dashboards will stay sane.

Kevin Osei: pls include guidance on `user_agent` normalization in exporter docs too (customers ask).

Allison Grant: noted. i’ll update the draft and link in this thread once pushed. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: thanks. let’s lock naming this week so instrumentation doesn’t churn.

title: #architecture: 1737052880
preview:
Ethan Park: Kicking off the ADR discussion for optimization config versioning. Core question: do we make configs mutable with an “active” pointer, or immutable versions with promotion/rollback semantics on top? Want to land a decision this week so ENG-4821/4825 can stop thrashing.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also need to be crisp about what “promotion” means. Is it a state on the config object, or a separate record of “this scope is currently pinned to version X”? Those are very different for audit/RBAC.

Benji Okafor: Option A (mutable config): single config row per scope (tenant/model/region), updates in place. Rollback = write old values back. Fast/simple, but diff history becomes “best effort” unless we snapshot. Also concurrency gets gross.

Michael Nguyen: The “mutable” approach means we basically reinvent versioning via audit table anyway. We’ll need immutable snapshots for diff view + rollback reliability. Otherwise you can’t guarantee rollback is byte-for-byte to a known-good.

Noah Patel: From runtime side, please don’t do in-place mutation. Mixed worker states become harder to reason about. If each apply references a `config_version_id`, the applier can be idempotent (`already_applied(version_id) => noop`). With in-place mutation, you need content hashing + you still have races if the same ID changes out from under you.

Chloe Martin: Console angle: users expect to see a timeline: “v12 created by Benji, canary applied to us-east 10%, promoted to 100%, rolled back to v11”. That’s easiest when versions are immutable. “Promoted” feels like an event, not a property.

Ethan Park: So likely Option B: immutable `OptimizationConfigVersion` objects. But then how do we represent what’s “active”? A pointer per scope?

Jordan Lee: I’m leaning toward a separate assignment object. Like `OptimizationConfigBinding` (name TBD):
- scope keys: tenant_id, model, region (or allow wildcards later)
- env: hosted/dedicated/private? (or “deployment_id”)
- active_version_id
- updated_at, updated_by
And keep a history table of binding changes.

Benji Okafor: That’s basically: versions are content-addressed snapshots, bindings are the mutable part. Promotion = update binding in a “wider” scope or environment. Rollback = update binding back to a prior version. Makes sense.

Marcus Lin: Security hat: if “promotion” is a status field on the version (e.g., `status=PROD`), then you conflate “this version is prod *somewhere*” with access control. RBAC wants: who can change prod binding for tenant X vs who can create a version draft. Separate binding gives us clean permission boundaries.

Connor O'Brien: Release/process angle: if you put “prod” on the version, you’ll get weirdness in multi-region rollouts. One region could be on v12 while another is on v11, but version says PROD… which is it? With bindings, “prod-ue1 binding points to v12” is unambiguous.

Ethan Park: Counterpoint: binding tables can explode (tenant*model*region). We’ll need reasonable defaults/inheritance.

Jordan Lee: We can do a hierarchy later (global -> tenant -> tenant+model -> tenant+model+region). But even with just “exact scope only” v1, bindings still exist. The ADR can state we’re not solving inheritance initially.

Michael Nguyen: Storage-wise, bindings won’t be that bad if we’re only storing for opted-in scopes. Also we can compress via “scope string” like `tenant:abc/model:llama3-70b/region:us-east-1` and index it, but that’s implementation.

Noah Patel: Another runtime reason: on startup we can ask control plane “what binding applies to me?” and get a single `active_version_id`. Workers cache by version ID. If bindings mutate, workers still just see “active_version_id changed from 11 -> 12” and do a safe transaction.

Chloe Martin: UI question: do we show “promotion” as a lifecycle state on the version (Draft/Validated/Active/etc) or purely as events? Users like statuses.

Benji Okafor: We can have version metadata like `created`, `validated`, `validation_errors` but not “active”. “Active” depends on binding/scope.

Jordan Lee: +1. Make “validated” a property of version (result of semantic validation), but “applied/promoted/rolled back” are binding events.

Marcus Lin: Also audit: event taxonomy becomes cleaner:
- optimization_config_version.created
- optimization_config_version.validated
- optimization_config_binding.updated (apply/promote/rollback can just be reason codes)
Then SIEM queries are consistent.

Ethan Park: Ok, but product/ops wants explicit “promote” and “rollback” verbs. Can we preserve those semantics in the API even if under the hood it’s “update binding”?

Benji Okafor: Yep. API can expose endpoints:
- POST /versions (create)
- POST /versions/{id}/validate
- POST /bindings/{scope}/apply {version_id, rollout_policy}
- POST /bindings/{scope}/rollback {to_version_id}

Connor O'Brien: Please keep rollback as a first-class endpoint for tooling + incident bot. Even if it maps to “binding update,” the semantics differ (e.g., requires confirmation + emits rollback audit event + maybe enforces cooldown).

Noah Patel: One more thing: should rollback create a *new* version that is identical to old version, or re-point to the old version?

Michael Nguyen: Re-point. Creating a new identical version breaks “known-good” identity. Also diff/history becomes confusing (“why does v13 equal v11?”).

Jordan Lee: Agree. If someone needs to make a small edit, they create v14. Rollback should be a binding change referencing existing version.

Chloe Martin: UX: timeline entry “Rolled back from v12 -> v11” is very legible. If we create v13 identical, it looks like we shipped a new config rather than reverting.

Marcus Lin: Audit requirements also favor re-pointing. Rollback event should include `from_version_id` and `to_version_id`, plus `reason` and `incident_id` optionally.

Ethan Park: What about the argument that “rollback is a change” so you want an immutable record of *the action*? With re-pointing, the immutable record is the audit event + binding history row.

Jordan Lee: Exactly. Binding history is append-only (or at least we treat it as immutable) so we have the action log.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: we should make binding updates conditional (compare-and-swap) to avoid the apply/rollback race. Like `UPDATE bindings SET active_version_id=? WHERE scope=? AND active_version_id=?` and fail if someone else changed it.

Noah Patel: +1000. Runtime applier will also do CAS on “current applied version” locally. But control plane must guard too.

Connor O'Brien: Can we capture in ADR that “promotion” is not a version state, it’s a binding update potentially across a broader scope? This will matter when we do staged rollouts.

Ethan Park: Yes. Drafting ADR language:
1) Versions are immutable snapshots with metadata + validation results.
2) Active config per scope is determined by a separate binding (mutable) with full history.
3) Promotion/rollback are binding changes with explicit audit events.
4) Runtime consumes version IDs for idempotency.

Chloe Martin: Add: Console will label “Active in X scopes” by querying bindings; don’t store it on the version.

Marcus Lin: Please explicitly call out RBAC split:
- create version: optimize_config.version.create
- validate: optimize_config.version.validate
- update binding (apply/promote/rollback): optimize_config.binding.update with reason codes; prod requires stronger permission.

Jordan Lee: Also call out scoping semantics v1: no inheritance, no wildcard. Otherwise we’ll bikeshed another week.

Benji Okafor: Works for me. I’ll add a note that later we can support “environment aliases” (e.g., stable/latest) as bindings, but those are still bindings.

Noah Patel: One last runtime note: if bindings are the mutable pointer, please ensure we can fetch “current binding” and “version payload” separately with ETags. We want low-latency polling and avoid pulling full config each time.

Michael Nguyen: Yep, versions table can be cached aggressively; binding fetch is small. Also, binding history should be append-only to support the diff view and “who changed what.”

Ethan Park: Cool. I’ll update ADR draft with this decision and circulate for :eyes: today. If no strong objections by EOD tomorrow, we mark immutable+binding as chosen.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: please tag me when the ADR is ready; release checklist depends on these semantics.

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: will align with Kenji on wording for “apply vs promote” in UI once ADR is locked.

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: security approves this direction. Just make sure rollback emits a distinct event name (not just “binding updated”) so customer audit queries are sane.

Jordan Lee: +1. We can keep “binding.updated” as the base event but also emit “binding.rolled_back” (or include action=ROLLBACK). Let’s make it explicit.

Ethan Park: Got it. Action item: I’ll propose explicit action enum + include `request_id` + `actor` in event payload. End thread.

title: #architecture: 1736112289
preview:
Ravi Menon: Dropping ADR-047 here for arch review. Focus areas we need eyes on: deterministic assignment inputs (hashing + normalization), default stickiness behavior when ids are missing, and cross-region determinism. Goal is “same request context -> same variant” regardless of router instance/region.

Jordan Lee: Read through. Overall 👍. Biggest thing: we need to be super explicit on the hash inputs + canonicalization or we’ll get “it’s deterministic in Go but not in TS” bugs later.

Jordan Lee: Proposal: canonical byte string `account_id|experiment_id|route_id|sticky_key` (pipe delim, UTF-8), then `xxh3_64` (or xxhash64) -> uint64 -> bucket via modulo. The important bit is never allow locale/JSON/stringify differences.

Ethan Park: +1 on canonicalization. Also please include config version or not? If we include config version, variant assignment will shift on every edit.

Jordan Lee: I would NOT include config_version in the hash. Determinism should be stable across config propagation lag. If weights change, assignment distribution changes anyway, but same hash value should map predictably based on current weights.

Jada Williams: Re: weight change behavior — we need to document that weight edits will cause some users to “move” variants (unless they keep the same cumulative intervals). People will ask “why did user flip?”

Ravi Menon: Yeah ADR text currently says “deterministic given (experiment config + hash input)”. We can add a note that changing allocations re-partitions the hash space.

Mina Haddad: On cross-region: do we guarantee the same bucketing across us-east + eu-west if the same sticky key is used? (I think we want yes.) That means: same hash algo, same normalization, same salt, and the policy evaluator must use normalized route ids (templates) not raw paths.

Allison Grant: +1000 on route id normalization. If we use raw URL path (e.g. `/v1/chat/123`), cardinality + assignment will be chaotic. Please ensure the ADR references route template id or stable route_id, not request path.

Ravi Menon: ADR currently uses `route_id` from policy engine (the normalized/templated identifier). I’ll add an explicit “never raw path” callout.

Vikram Singh: Missing identifier behavior: what happens when no stickiness key is provided? The ADR mentions fallback to “best available identifier”, but we should be careful: IP address is a footgun (NAT, mobile) and can cause weird skew.

Jordan Lee: Agree. My preference order (if provided):
1) `x-redwood-sticky-key` (explicit)
2) `end_user_id` (if customer sends it)
3) `session_id` (SDK-generated, if stable)
4) If none: non-sticky assignment (random) + emit a warning header / metric (`experiment_sticky_key_missing=1`).

Jada Williams: +1 “non-sticky” default. Otherwise we’ll end up with the low-entropy bug we saw in earlier prototypes (everyone bucketing on `unknown` -> variant skew).

Ethan Park: If non-sticky, make sure we’re still deterministic per request retry? e.g. streaming retries should not flip variants mid-request.

Jordan Lee: Yeah, the “non-sticky” can still be deterministic at request scope by using request_id as sticky key *within a single request lifecycle* (request_id is propagated across retries). But we shouldn’t pretend it’s user-sticky.

Mina Haddad: That’s good, but careful: if request_id changes on retry, you’ll flip. Need to ensure the retry path reuses the same request_id or has a stable “attempt group id”.

Ravi Menon: We already have a retry “span link id” and request context id. I’ll add a sentence: “When no sticky key, use request_context_id (stable across retries) to keep intra-request consistency.”

Marcus Lin: Security/abuse angle: if the assignment key is user-controllable (header), a customer could brute force keys to force a variant. Is that a concern? For most A/B tests it’s fine, but some customers will care.

Jordan Lee: We can mitigate by mixing in `account_id` and `experiment_id` always. That prevents cross-tenant influence, but doesn’t prevent a customer from selecting a variant within their own tenant.

Marcus Lin: That’s the main requirement (tenant isolation). For customers who care, they can avoid exposing the sticky key to end users and generate it server-side. Maybe mention “do not use untrusted client input” in docs later.

Allison Grant: Hash function choice: I’d avoid anything with language-specific implementations that differ in endianness. `HMAC-SHA256` is easiest to standardize across languages, though slower. `xxhash` is fine if we lock versions and provide reference code.

Jordan Lee: Performance-wise, HMAC on every request in the hot path might be non-trivial. I think `xxh3_64` is good and we can provide a tiny shared lib (Go + Rust), SDKs just pass the sticky key, not compute. The evaluator is in policy engine (Go), so only one impl really matters.

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: determinism failures are brutal to debug. If we go with xxhash, please include in ADR: log/debug endpoint should show the exact preimage string + hash output + chosen interval. Otherwise oncall will be blind.

Ravi Menon: 👍 We have a debug endpoint PR planned; I’ll add required fields: `hash_input_components`, `canonical_string`, `hash64`, `bucket`, `weights`, `variant_selected`, `config_etag`.

Vikram Singh: Allocation math: please don’t use floating percentages. Use integers (basis points) to avoid rounding differences. Like weights sum to 10,000.

Jordan Lee: Yup. ADR currently says “percent split”. I’ll update to “allocation in basis points (0-10,000), validated to sum exactly 10,000”.

Mina Haddad: Cross-region config drift: even with deterministic hash, if eu-west is on config vN and us-east is on vN+1, same user will get different variant. We should be clear we’re solving “same inputs, same code” not “eventual consistency”.

Ethan Park: That’s where control-plane SLO comes in (propagation lag). But ADR should mention “consistency depends on config propagation”.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also, if config is missing/unavailable in a region, do we default to control variant? That’s “safe” but can bias results.

Jada Williams: Bias is acceptable vs routing errors. For experiment safety, defaulting to baseline/control when config missing seems right.

Jordan Lee: Agree: if evaluator can’t fetch config, fall back to “no experiment applied” (control route) and emit metric `experiment_config_unavailable=1`.

Allison Grant: One more: salt. If we ever need to re-key hashing to fix a bug, salt lets us version the bucketing algorithm without changing the whole input structure. Suggest include `bucketing_salt` (per experiment) stored in config.

Jordan Lee: I’m okay with a per-experiment salt, but default it to stable server-generated on experiment create. Don’t let users edit it (or they’ll accidentally reshuffle everyone).

Ravi Menon: Noted. Will add: `salt` is generated at create-time, immutable, and included in canonical string.

Marcus Lin: If salt is stored, treat it as non-secret (it’s not security), but do include it in audit logs when created.

Ravi Menon: Cool.

Ravi Menon: Summary of requested ADR tweaks:
- Define canonical hash preimage exactly: `account_id|experiment_id|route_id|salt|sticky_key` (UTF-8, pipe delim)
- Use integer basis points allocations (sum=10,000)
- Stickiness precedence: explicit header first; no IP fallback; if missing -> request-scoped deterministic (request_context_id) and emit warning
- Cross-region determinism: require normalized route_id (template), and same evaluator implementation; call out config propagation caveat
- Failure modes: config unavailable -> treat as no experiment/control + emit metrics
- Debuggability: required debug fields to inspect hash inputs/output

Ravi Menon: If no objections, I’ll fold these into ADR-047 and mark “approved” in Confluence.

Jordan Lee: Sounds good from me.

Allison Grant: +1 with the route_id wording change.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Please tag me on the debug endpoint spec once it’s written.

Marcus Lin: 👍

Ethan Park: Approved.

Jada Williams: 👍

Mina Haddad: 👍

title: #eng-ml: 1719509999
preview:
Aisha Khan: quick follow-up on the precision explainability RFC that landed this morning — we still need to decide ownership and the deprecation/playbook for old quant profiles. main reqs i think: per-response explainability fields (quality_delta estimate, profile_id, fallback_reason), stable header for client compatibility, and a migration cadence. who wants to own the playbook?

Ben Liu: do we expect the runtime to compute the `quality_delta` at inference time or should that be produced offline by eval and attached via profile metadata?

Clara Martinez: my leaning is runtime-provided, but only an estimate. we can use cached calibration maps produced offline + a small runtime heuristic. example config sketch: 

```
precision_profile:
  id: "fp8-lite-v2"
  explain_mode: runtime
  explain_fields: ["quality_delta_est","fallback_reason","profile_id"]
  expose_headers: ["X-Quant-Profile","X-Quality-Est"]
```

Clara Martinez: runtime estimate keeps clients simple (single response) and lets ops emit per-request telemetry. offline eval still calibrates the maps used for the estimate.

Dan O'Neill: +1 but watch backward compat. older clients will expect the old JSON schema. propose we always include `explain` field as nullable object and keep top-level tokens same. also add a stable response header `X-Quant-Profile: fp8-lite-v2` so proxies and SRE can route.

Ethan Park: for observability i want a single histogram metric `redwood.quant.quality_delta` tagged by `profile_id`, `model`, `customer_id` (where allowed). alert: quality-regression if p95 > threshold for 24h. also we should surface a TTL'd daily rollout delta for new profiles.

Clara Martinez: privacy note — scrub prompt/response PII before attaching any per-request explainability blob to telemetry. probably do hash-only customer ids unless the tenant opted-in.

Aisha Khan: agreed on privacy. deprecation cadence proposal: 90-day notice + 30-day soft-fallback window where runtime serves a compatibility shim that adds `explain` = null + header, and telemetry still records legacy metrics. after 120 days auto-redirect to next-best profile and mark former profile deprecated in catalog UI. we should make rollback easy for first 2 weeks.

Ben Liu: i can own the playbook draft. action items: 1) draft playbook (who, cadence, rollback), 2) runtime impl spec for `quality_delta_est`, 3) telemetry labels and alert thresholds, 4) API compatibility contract. i'll ping folks in this thread when draft ready.

deploy-bot: deploy succeeded for branch `quant-explainability/shim` — id 1719509999.1 :white_check_mark:

Aisha Khan: awesome, thanks @Ben Liu. i'll add product + legal to review the privacy bits and then we can schedule a short canary next week. action: Ben to create PR for playbook, Clara to open runtime spec PR, Ethan to propose alert thresholds.

Ben Liu: filing PR today, thanks all :rocket:

title: #eng-ml: 1711401234
preview:
Ethan Park: Heads-up idea: for Dedicated/Private customers we should sign quant profiles (profile provenance) so runtime can verify origin and prevent accidental unsafe overrides.
Nina Gomez: signing sounds good for compliance. What would we sign exactly? whole profile blob or a canonical digest?
Ethan Park: canonical digest + metadata (author, calibration_version, signature_time). Keep blobs in artifact store; runtime fetches signed manifest.
Lukas Weber: runtime cost: verifying a signature per-deploy is cheap, but per-request would be too slow. verify at load-time and cache.
Nina Gomez: also need an audit trail: who approved the profile, which eval anchors passed. tie to ML-rollback playbook.
Ethan Park: proposal: profile lifecycle: draft -> signed-for-staging -> staging-eval -> signed-for-production. Each sign step requires lint + anchor checks.
Lukas Weber: deploy-time checks: cert root rotation, signature expiry, and tenant allowlist. If signature invalid, runtime falls back to tenant-default safe profile.
Nina Gomez: product ask: allow customers to bring their own signing key (BYOK) for private. Provide verification hooks for on-prem.
Ethan Park: ops: store short provenance header in request logs: profile_id, signer_email, sig_version. keep it compact to not bloat logs.
Lukas Weber: telemetry: add provenance fields in per-deploy metrics so we can attribute regressions to profile versions. Also postmortem search by signer.
Nina Gomez: security: threat model - signed but malicious profile. Mitigate: require two-person sign for any profile that downgrades precision beyond threshold, and safety_critical ops require manual approval.
Ethan Park: runtime auto-revert rule: if production profile causes anchor-diff > threshold or human-eval drop, auto-revert to previous signed profile and alert.
ops-bot: Created ticket ML-4310: "Quant profile provenance & signing flow" assigned to Ethan Park, Nina Gomez, Lukas Weber. https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/ML-4310 :white_check_mark:
Nina Gomez: next steps: 1) spec manifest format + signing fields (Nina). 2) runtime: load-time verify + cache + auto-revert (Lukas). 3) ML: lint/signing gates + two-person approval (Ethan).
Ethan Park: timeline: spec this week, PoC load-time verify next sprint. Good meeting, thanks.

title: #design: 1708459123
preview:
Ethan Park: Quick thread from the QA pass: modal stacking + toast placement are causing visual overlap on narrow dashboards. Need a decision on z-index and spacing tokens before we cut the release.
Nadia Ruiz: I saw that. When a modal opens while a toast is active, the toast can sit above the modal header on mobile. Looks wrong.
Aria Singh: We should standardize layer tokens: `--z-surface`, `--z-overlay`, `--z-toast`. Proposed ordering: surface < toast < overlay (modal). That keeps toasts under modals.
Tom Brooks: I worry about toasts that are meant to be foreground (e.g., "Something failed" that needs attention). If we hide them under modals they might be missed. Any UX tradeoffs?
Ethan Park: Good point. Maybe toasts that are critical can be promoted with `data-toast-priority="high"` to sit above overlays. Default toasts remain below modals.
Nadia Ruiz: Agree. Also spacing: toasts currently sit flush with top edge of modal when overlay is present. Can we introduce `--toast-margin-from-overlay: 12px` so they're visually separated?
Aria Singh: Sounds good. I'll add tokens: 
```
--z-surface: 0;
--z-toast: 400;
--z-overlay: 600;
--toast-margin-from-overlay: 12px;
--toast-compact-gap: 8px;
```
Tom Brooks: One more nit from accessibility review: keyboard focus order when opening a modal with an active toast. Focus should go to the modal first, not the toast. QA-bot flagged a case where a persistent toast grabbed focus.
QA-bot: Automated check: "focus-trap" failed 1/12 on /dashboard with toast persistent=true. See logs: https://ci.internal/redwood/1234
Ethan Park: We'll enforce that toasts are inert for focus when overlay exists unless promoted. Add `aria-hidden="true"` on default toasts when overlay present.
Nadia Ruiz: Also copy tweak for compact dashboard toast: current wording "Saved" is ambiguous when multiple saves happen. Suggest "Preferences saved" or include scope: "Dashboard settings saved."
Aria Singh: Yeah include scope. I'll update i18n keys: `toast.saved.scope=Dashboard settings saved.`
Tom Brooks: Spacing tokens question: should we have a dedicated token for modal header padding vs body padding? Some modules want denser headers.
Ethan Park: Let's introduce `--modal-header-padding` and `--modal-body-padding`, defaulting to 16px and 20px respectively. That gives designers granularity without overriding global surface tokens.
Aria Singh: CSS snippet I'll land: 
```
.modal { z-index: var(--z-overlay); }
.toast { z-index: var(--z-toast); margin-top: var(--toast-margin-from-overlay); }
.overlay-active .toast { aria-hidden: true; }
.modal__header { padding: var(--modal-header-padding); }
.modal__body { padding: var(--modal-body-padding); }
```
Nadia Ruiz: Small micro-interaction ask: when a modal opens, we fade out table row hover highlights under it. Use `--underlay-hover-opacity: 0.6`.
Tom Brooks: That helps. Also ensure focus ring for buttons inside compact modals doesn't exceed modal padding. QA found the ring clipped on small screens.
Ethan Park: We'll increase the padding or adjust outline-offset for compact variant: `--button-focus-offset: 3px` for compact.
QA-bot: Style regression checklist updated: modal z-index and toast behavior flagged for visual tests.
design-bot: Created draft PR: design/modal-toast-layering and added tokens file preview at design/tokens/layering.yml
Aria Singh: I'll include examples for promoting a toast: `<div class="toast" data-toast-priority="high" aria-hidden="false">` in docs.
Nadia Ruiz: I'll update the accessibility note in the component docs to call out focus behavior and the `data-toast-priority` exception.
Tom Brooks: Thanks. If we can get the PR in by EOD we can queue for the patch release.
Ethan Park: Great work team. I'll add the action items to the PR description and tag product for copy confirmation. :rocket:
